ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for Bò Lúc Lắc Khoai Tây; mapped to broader 'beef dish' class (FOODON_03311737). A more specific term for Vietnamese shaking beef with fried potatoes is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef meat dish — stir-fried beef with potato (FoodEx2 broad category for cooked beef-based mixed dish)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Bò Lúc Lắc Khoai Tây. A00GJ (meat dish) is used as the closest defensible broader anchor. Specific Vietnamese stir-fried beef with French fries is not enumerated in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Vietnamese beef and French fries dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual ethnic dish preparations at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Bò Lúc Lắc Khoai Tây is approximately 300g, comprising a portion of stir-fried marinated beef cubes alongside a serving of deep-fried French fries, plated together as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: Bò Lúc Lắc Khoai Tây (Stir Fried Beef With French Fries) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bò Lúc Lắc Khoai Tây (Stir Fried Beef With French Fries), record G00010555, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1 (data not available) or estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous components where noted.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of French Fries and Potato Products — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bò Lúc Lắc Khoai Tây as a composite dish. The GI of 75 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate component: deep-fried French fries, which have a reported GI of approximately 75–85 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load (Diabetes Care 44(Suppl 1):S1–S13). A midpoint GI of 75 is applied conservatively. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 39.5 × 3.0) / 100 = 88.9 for a 300g serving (carbohydrate content 118.5g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 618mg/100g — exceeds the 600mg/100g high-concern threshold; driven by oyster sauce, soy sauce, and fish sauce in the marinade
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its sodium content of 618mg/100g; if consumed, request reduced marinade sauce and substitute butter with a plant-based oil to also lower saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI deep-fried French fries (GI ~75) contribute 39.5g carbohydrate/100g with a carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio of approximately 10:1, producing a high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, replace French fries with boiled or steamed potatoes to reduce glycemic impact, significantly reduce portion size, and request reduced sugar in the marinade.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat estimated at 0.2g/100g from deep-frying, saturated fat approximately 3.5g/100g from beef and butter, and sodium 618mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to the combination of trans fat from deep-fried fries, saturated fat from beef and butter, and high sodium from the marinade; if consumed occasionally, request air-fried or baked potato substitutes and reduced-sodium marinade.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (~0.2g/100g) from deep-frying, pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid in red beef, and advanced glycation end products (AGEs) generated by deep-frying collectively worsen joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; deep-fried preparation generates trans fat and AGEs that are strongly pro-inflammatory, beef is high in arachidonic acid, and both tomatoes and potatoes are nightshades that may trigger individual RA flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bò Lúc Lắc Khoai Tây, known in English as Stir-Fried Beef with French Fries, is a Vietnamese-origin dish that has become popular in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurant scene. The dish combines two distinct preparations: tender cubes of marinated beef that are stir-fried at high heat using a characteristic 'shaking' wok technique (lúc lắc means 'to shake'), and a side of deep-fried French fries made from potato. The beef is typically marinated in a mixture of oyster sauce, soy sauce, fish sauce, garlic, and sometimes sugar, then stir-fried in butter or oil until caramelised on the outside and medium-rare inside. The French fries are deep-fried separately until golden and crispy. The dish is served with fresh tomatoes, onion rings, and sometimes a dipping sauce of lime juice and black pepper. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 290 kcal/100g, with a moderate fat content of 13.2g/100g and a high carbohydrate load of 39.5g/100g dominated by the French fries. Sodium is elevated at 618mg/100g due to the multi-sauce marinade. The dish is not suitable for patients with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. The glycemic index is high (~75) due to the deep-fried potato component. Potassium is notable at 538mg/100g from the potato content. This dish is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner main course in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record G00010555) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous components (beef + fried potato) and documented accordingly. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for French fries as the dominant carbohydrate component.
notes: sugar_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record. This is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB database entry, noting that the marinade sugar content may be minimal and below detection threshold in the composite dish as prepared. Saturated fat of 3.5g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for stir-fried beef (approximately 2.0g/100g) and deep-fried French fries (approximately 1.5g/100g) weighted by approximate composition ratio, as the HPB record does not report saturated fat separately. Trans fat of 0.2g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried potato products, consistent with the health_suitability input data. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Vietnamese beef and fries dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry as of 2024. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish, and no sufficiently close composite-dish equivalent exists in USDA FoodData Central to support reliable estimation for this specific preparation as of 2024. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 300g serving: GL = (75 × 118.5) / 100 = 88.9. This dish is served in Singapore Vietnamese restaurants and is tagged to region SG. The dish is not halal-certified as standard due to the use of beef without confirmed halal slaughter certification and the presence of oyster sauce which may contain non-halal additives; halal status should be verified per restaurant.
